# Sealed Secrets — pf9-mngt
#
# These encrypted YAML files are generated by `kubeseal` against your
# cluster's Sealed Secrets controller public key.
#
# They are SAFE to commit — they can only be decrypted by the Sealed Secrets
# controller running in YOUR cluster (kube-system/sealed-secrets-controller).
# If someone clones this repo they get useless encrypted blobs.
#
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# HOW TO GENERATE (run on the cluster control plane or any machine with
# kubectl access and kubeseal installed):
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# Step 1 — Install kubeseal CLI (matching the controller version):
#   KUBESEAL_VERSION=$(kubectl get deployment sealed-secrets-controller \
#     -n kube-system -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}' \
#     | cut -d: -f2)
#   wget "https://github.com/bitnami-labs/sealed-secrets/releases/download/${KUBESEAL_VERSION}/kubeseal-$(uname -s | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')-amd64.tar.gz" -O - | tar xz
#   sudo mv kubeseal /usr/local/bin/
#
# Step 2 — Generate each sealed secret (replace placeholder values with real ones):
#
#   # 1. PostgreSQL password
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-db-credentials \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=password='REPLACE_WITH_REAL_DB_PASSWORD' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > db-credentials.yaml
#
#   # 2. JWT secret key (generate with: python3 -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(64))")
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-jwt-secret \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=jwt-secret-key='REPLACE_WITH_64_CHAR_HEX_STRING' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > jwt-secret.yaml
#
#   # 3. Web admin credentials (the admin username/password you use to log in to the UI)
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-admin-credentials \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=admin-password='REPLACE_WITH_ADMIN_UI_PASSWORD' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > admin-credentials.yaml
#
#   # 4. LDAP secrets
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-ldap-secrets \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=admin-password='REPLACE_WITH_LDAP_ADMIN_PASS' \
#     --from-literal=config-password='REPLACE_WITH_LDAP_CONFIG_PASS' \
#     --from-literal=readonly-password='REPLACE_WITH_LDAP_READONLY_PASS' \
#     --from-literal=sync-key='REPLACE_WITH_LDAP_SYNC_KEY' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > ldap-secrets.yaml
#
#   # 4. SMTP password (omit or use dummy if email is disabled)
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-smtp-secrets \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=password='REPLACE_WITH_SMTP_PASSWORD' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > smtp-secrets.yaml
#
#   # 5. Platform9 credentials (main service account)
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-pf9-credentials \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=password='REPLACE_WITH_PF9_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_PASSWORD' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > pf9-credentials.yaml
#
#   # 6. Snapshot service user (snapshotsrv)
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-snapshot-creds \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=password-key='REPLACE_WITH_SNAPSHOT_PASSWORD_KEY' \
#     --from-literal=user-password-encrypted='REPLACE_WITH_SNAPSHOT_USER_PASSWORD_ENCRYPTED' \
#     --from-literal=service-user-email='REPLACE_WITH_SNAPSHOT_SERVICE_USER_EMAIL' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > snapshot-creds.yaml
#
#   # 7. VM provisioning service user (provisionsrv)
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-provision-creds \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=password-key='REPLACE_WITH_PROVISION_PASSWORD_KEY' \
#     --from-literal=user-password-encrypted='REPLACE_WITH_PROVISION_USER_PASSWORD_ENCRYPTED' \
#     --from-literal=service-user-email='REPLACE_WITH_PROVISION_SERVICE_USER_EMAIL' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > provision-creds.yaml
#
#   # 8. SSH credentials (optional — leave password blank if not used)
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-ssh-credentials \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=password='REPLACE_WITH_SSH_PASSWORD_OR_EMPTY' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > ssh-credentials.yaml
#
#   # 9. Copilot/AI keys (optional — leave blank if not using AI features)
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-copilot-secrets \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=openai-api-key='' \
#     --from-literal=anthropic-api-key='' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > copilot-secrets.yaml
#
#   # 10. Encryption keys for secrets at rest (v1.83.25+)
#   #     vm-provision-key: Fernet key for encrypting VM OS passwords in vm_provisioning_vms
#   #     smtp-config-key:  Fernet key for encrypting SMTP password in system_settings
#   #     Generate each with: python3 -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))"
#   #     Use DIFFERENT random values for each key — do not reuse keys across secrets.
#   kubectl create secret generic pf9-encryption-secrets \
#     --namespace pf9-mngt \
#     --from-literal=vm-provision-key='REPLACE_WITH_RANDOM_HEX_64_CHARS' \
#     --from-literal=smtp-config-key='REPLACE_WITH_DIFFERENT_RANDOM_HEX_64_CHARS' \
#     --dry-run=client -o yaml \
#   | kubeseal --namespace pf9-mngt --format yaml > encryption-secrets.yaml
#
# Step 3 — Apply all sealed secrets to the cluster:
#   kubectl apply -f sealed-secrets/ -n pf9-mngt
#
# Step 4 — Verify (should show all 10 secrets as regular Secrets):
#   kubectl get secrets -n pf9-mngt
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# IMPORTANT: The .yaml files in this directory are generated by kubeseal
# and will be committed to this private repo. Run the commands above on
# your cluster to populate them.
